mnemonics.title: DISK SCHEDULING ALGORITHMS
mnemonics.content: F S S C - FIRST SERVE SHORTEST SCAN CYCLIC: FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN (order of complexity and algorithm type)

mnemonics.title: FILE ALLOCATION METHODS
mnemonics.content: CIA - CONTIGUOUS INDEXED ALLOCATED: The three main file allocation methods in order of complexity

mnemonics.title: DISK GEOMETRY COMPONENTS
mnemonics.content: T S C P - TRACK SECTOR CYLINDER PLATTER: Remember disk structure components

mnemonics.title: DIRECTORY OPERATIONS
mnemonics.content: CRDL O S - CREATE DELETE LIST OPEN SEARCH: Main directory operations

mnemonics.title: BOOT PROCESS ORDER
mnemonics.content: BOOT SUPER INODE DATA: Boot block, Superblock, Inode table, Data blocks (file system layout order)

acronyms.usage: Simplest disk scheduling algorithm that processes requests in order of arrival
Disk scheduling that selects request closest to current head position

acronyms.expansion: Disk Head Moves in Both Directions
acronyms.usage: Elevator algorithm that services requests in one direction then reverses
Disk scheduling that services requests in one direction and jumps back to start
Similar to SCAN but only goes as far as the last request in each direction
Data structure that stores information about a file (metadata and block pointers)
Method of addressing disk sectors by logical sequence number rather than CHS

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an elevator in a building going from bottom to top collecting all passengers (requests), then going back down collecting remaining passengers - head moves like elevator servicing floor requests
Think of a parking lot with numbered spaces - each car (file) occupies consecutive spaces making it easy to find but creates gaps when cars leave (fragmentation)
Think of a treasure hunt clue list - each clue points to the next location; must follow sequentially but can use scattered spots anywhere
Think of a book table of contents - index page lists all chapter locations; can jump directly to any chapter without reading previous ones
Think of organizational chart or file cabinet with folders inside folders - tree structure with root at top and leaves at bottom

analogies.analogy: Like a table of contents in a book or an address book mapping names to phone numbers and addresses
Like a record player needle moving across vinyl to reach different songs - the further it travels, the longer it takes
Like a parking lot with empty and occupied spots viewed from above - green dots for empty, red for taken
Like a shortcut or alias on desktop pointing to a file stored elsewhere without duplicating the actual file
Like an index at the back of a book showing which pages contain related content, helping locate information quickly
